abstract: Another material object from which this material’s properties are derived.
overview:
## Discussion

When you use the [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial/property(with:)], [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial/propertyNamed(_:)], or [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial/subscript(_:)-323j3] method to access material properties, the [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial] class first checks whether the material itself has a material property with the specified name or semantic. If not, those methods forward to the corresponding method of the other [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial] object specified by this property.

Use this method to create sets of materials that share most of their material properties but differ in others. For example, you can have a set of objects in the same scene with the same general style of surface appearance (determined by the [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial/scatteringFunction] property) but with different values for the [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLScatteringFunction/baseColor] material property.

# base

**Instance Property**

Another material object from which this material’s properties are derived.

## Declaration

```swift
var base: MDLMaterial? { get set }
```

## Discussion

When you use the [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial/property(with:)], [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial/propertyNamed(_:)], or [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial/subscript(_:)-323j3] method to access material properties, the [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial] class first checks whether the material itself has a material property with the specified name or semantic. If not, those methods forward to the corresponding method of the other [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial] object specified by this property.

Use this method to create sets of materials that share most of their material properties but differ in others. For example, you can have a set of objects in the same scene with the same general style of surface appearance (determined by the [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLMaterial/scatteringFunction] property) but with different values for the [doc://com.apple.modelio/documentation/ModelIO/MDLScatteringFunction/baseColor] material property.
